questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must a salesperson candidate complete in New Jersey?
questions.explanation: New Jersey requires 75 hours of pre-license education for salesperson candidates. The 90-hour figure is the general course requirement for brokers, not salespersons. Broker candidates need a total of 150 hours including two additional 30-hour courses.

questions.question_text: A candidate wants to upgrade to a broker license. What is the total pre-license education requirement?
questions.explanation: New Jersey broker candidates must complete 150 hours of education, consisting of a 90-hour general broker course plus two 30-hour elective courses. The 75-hour requirement applies to salespersons only, not broker candidates.

questions.question_text: A salesperson candidate completed her 75-hour course 14 months ago but has not taken the exam. What is her status?
distractors: She can still sit for the exam
questions.explanation: Exam eligibility expires one year after completion of pre-license education. At 14 months, the candidate's eligibility has lapsed. She must retake the pre-license course to establish new eligibility. There are no automatic extensions for the one-year window.

questions.question_text: How is a candidate's exam eligibility transmitted to the testing provider after completing pre-license education?
questions.explanation: Eligibility to sit for the PSI exam is transmitted electronically from the approved education provider. Candidates do not need to mail transcripts or have their broker submit documentation. This electronic process streamlines exam scheduling.

questions.question_text: A broker candidate's education includes a 90-hour general course. What additional coursework is required?
questions.explanation: Broker candidates must complete the 90-hour general broker course plus two separate 30-hour courses, totaling 150 hours. The courses are not consolidated into a single 60-hour class. There is no requirement for three 20-hour specialty courses.

questions.question_text: Which testing vendor administers the New Jersey real estate licensing exam?
questions.explanation: PSI is the contracted vendor that administers the New Jersey real estate licensing examination. Pearson VUE and Prometric administer exams for other professions or states. ETS is primarily known for academic testing, not professional licensing.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the PSI exam and wants to apply for her license. What must she secure first?
questions.explanation: New Jersey requires salesperson candidates to secure an employing broker before applying for their license. The license application requires broker sponsorship. E&O insurance, bank accounts, and MLS membership are not prerequisites for the license application.

questions.question_text: A license applicant asks why fingerprinting is required as part of the NJ application process. What is its primary purpose?
questions.correct_answer: To conduct a criminal background check
distractors: To verify the applicant's identity at testing
To link the license to a biometric record
To confirm completion of pre-license education
questions.explanation: Fingerprinting is required for NJ real estate license applicants to facilitate a criminal background check. This protects the public by screening applicants before they are licensed. Identity verification at testing and education confirmation are handled through separate processes.

questions.question_text: A candidate's employment date with a broker is listed as two weeks before the exam date. What problem does this create?
questions.correct_answer: Employment date cannot precede the exam date
distractors: No problem; early dates are acceptable
The broker must also retake the exam
questions.explanation: New Jersey regulations require that the employment date cannot be prior to the exam date. Listing an employment start date before passing the exam would result in the application being rejected. The broker does not need to retake the exam, and no waiting period applies.

questions.question_text: A candidate passes the exam but waits 13 months to submit her license application. Her eligibility period was one year. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: She must retake the pre-license course and exam
distractors: She can still apply with a late fee
She only needs to retake the exam
questions.explanation: Because the one-year eligibility period has expired, the candidate cannot apply. She must complete the pre-license education again to establish new eligibility and then retake the PSI exam. There is no grace period or late-fee option to extend expired eligibility.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes a broker-salesperson from a broker in New Jersey?
questions.correct_answer: A broker-salesperson works under another broker
distractors: A broker-salesperson has fewer education hours
A broker-salesperson holds a salesperson license
questions.explanation: A broker-salesperson holds a broker license but chooses to work under another broker rather than operating independently. Both have the same education requirements. A broker-salesperson can perform all broker functions but does so under supervision of another broker.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed investor wants to wholesale residential contracts in New Jersey. Which license type applies?
questions.correct_answer: No license is required for wholesaling
questions.explanation: New Jersey has no wholesaling-specific license. Marketing and assigning your own equitable interest in a purchase contract does not require a real estate license. A 2022 bill (S3824) that would have created a 'Residential Property Wholesaler license' under the NJ Real Estate Commission died in committee and was never enacted. (Acting as a broker or agent for others for compensation still requires a license under N.J.S.A. 45:15-1 et seq.)

questions.question_text: A salesperson wants to operate her own brokerage. What license must she obtain?
questions.explanation: To operate an independent brokerage, an individual must hold a broker license. A broker-salesperson still works under another broker. There is no separate sole proprietor permit or upgraded salesperson license category in New Jersey.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT an actual New Jersey real estate license category?
questions.explanation: New Jersey issues three real estate license categories: broker, broker-salesperson, and salesperson (N.J.S.A. 45:15-9). There is no 'Residential Property Wholesaler' license — a 2022 bill (S3824) to create one under the NJ Real Estate Commission died in committee and was never enacted, so wholesaling a residential contract requires no special license.

questions.question_text: A person wants to teach pre-license courses in New Jersey. Which credential does she need from NJREC?
distractors: Salesperson license with three years experience
questions.explanation: NJREC issues a separate instructor license for individuals who want to teach approved real estate courses. A broker license alone does not authorize teaching. A state teaching certificate from the Department of Education is not a substitute for the NJREC instructor license.

questions.question_text: What is the total initial fee for a salesperson license in New Jersey?
questions.explanation: The total initial salesperson fee is $160, broken down as $100 license fee, $50 application fee, and $10 Guaranty Fund contribution. The $100 figure is only the license portion. $270 is the broker fee, and $200 is only the broker license portion.

questions.question_text: A broker renewing her license contributes $20 to the Guaranty Fund. A salesperson renewing contributes how much?
questions.explanation: Salespersons contribute $10 to the Guaranty Fund, while brokers contribute $20. These contributions fund the Real Estate Guaranty Fund that compensates consumers harmed by licensee misconduct. The amounts differ based on license level.

questions.question_text: How often must New Jersey real estate licenses be renewed?
questions.explanation: New Jersey real estate licenses are renewed on a two-year cycle, with the deadline falling on June 30 of the renewal year. Annual, three-year, and four-year cycles are used in other states but not in New Jersey.

questions.question_text: A disabled veteran applies for a salesperson license. What fee benefit does she receive?
questions.correct_answer: Fee waivers for the license application
distractors: A 50% discount on the exam fee
No benefits; all applicants pay the same
questions.explanation: New Jersey provides fee waivers for disabled veterans applying for real estate licenses. This is a state benefit intended to reduce barriers for veteran service members. Standard applicants do not receive this waiver, and it applies to license fees, not exam or CE costs.

questions.question_text: By what date must New Jersey licensees renew their licenses in each renewal cycle?
questions.explanation: New Jersey real estate licenses must be renewed by June 30 of the renewal year on a two-year cycle. Missing this deadline results in late fees and potential lapse of the license. The renewal period does not follow the calendar year end.

questions.question_text: How many continuing education credit hours must NJ licensees complete per renewal cycle?
questions.explanation: New Jersey requires 12 credit hours of continuing education per two-year licensing period. This is less than some neighboring states. The 12 hours must include specific mandatory topics covering ethics, agency, and fair housing.

questions.question_text: Which mandatory CE topics must be included in every renewal cycle?
questions.explanation: New Jersey mandates 2 hours of ethics, 1 hour of agency relationships (CPEA), and 1 hour of fair housing in every CE cycle. Contracts, property management, appraisal, and environmental law may be available as electives but are not mandatory topics.

questions.question_text: A salesperson licensed after July 1, 2024 is approaching her first renewal. How many CE hours must she complete?
questions.correct_answer: She is exempt for the first renewal term
distractors: 6 hours as a reduced first-term requirement
questions.explanation: New licensees whose initial license was issued after July 1, 2024 are exempt from CE requirements for their first renewal term. This gives new licensees time to gain practical experience before their CE obligation begins in the second cycle.

questions.question_text: A licensee fails to renew on time and applies two weeks late. What additional penalty applies?
questions.explanation: New Jersey imposes a $200 late fee for licensees who fail to renew by the June 30 deadline. There is no grace period that waives the fee. The late fee is in addition to all standard renewal fees that must still be paid.

questions.question_text: A broker licensed in Pennsylvania wants to practice in New Jersey based on her PA license. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, NJ does not offer license reciprocity
distractors: Yes, PA and NJ have full reciprocity
Yes, if she passes the NJ state portion only
questions.explanation: New Jersey does not have reciprocity agreements with any other state. A Pennsylvania licensee must complete all NJ pre-license education requirements, pass the full NJ exam, and apply for a NJ license independently. There are no temporary permits or partial exam waivers.
